title: P. succinatutens: Succinate Scavenging & Pathogen Inhibition
abstract: P. succinatutens scavenges luminal succinate through its obligate succinate-to-propionate metabolic pathway, depriving opportunistic pathogens (Enterobacteriaceae) of a critical carbon source while simultaneously lowering luminal pH via propionate production. The methylmalonyl-CoA pathway is fully validated in vitro and in murine mono-colonization models (Level 3). Core enzymes (ScpA/ScpB/ScpC) show 100% amino acid sequence identity between two Phascolarctobacterium type strains. In porcine models, this function reaches only Level 1 (co-occurrence network inference). Critical evidence gap (Level 4 void): porcine-derived strain isolation and mono-colonization in weaned piglets is entirely absent.
modelSystem: In vitro + Murine mono-colonization (L3); Porcine co-occurrence network (L1)
keyLimitation: No porcine-derived strain isolated; no mono-colonization in piglets performed. Translational bottleneck: murine L3 to porcine L4.
summary: Benchmark case for the translational gap. P. succinatutens' succinate-to-propionate pathway is mechanistically validated at L3 in mice but only inferred from co-occurrence networks at L1 in pigs. Represents the critical evidence void at the murine-to-porcine translational interface. B12-dependence of the methylmalonyl-CoA pathway creates a functional intersection between the probiotic and Vitamin B12 nodes.

title: P. succinatutens: Intestinal Barrier Maintenance via Propionate
abstract: P. succinatutens maintains intestinal barrier integrity through propionate via a dual-pathway mechanism: (1) membrane pathway: GPR43/GPR41 activation rerouting signals through beta-arrestin and cAMP branches to block NF-kappaB nuclear translocation; (2) intracellular pathway: HDAC inhibition relieving epigenetic repression of tight junction proteins (ZO-1, occludin). In murine models, the complete causal chain of mono-colonization to propionate production to TLR4/NF-kappaB inhibition to barrier maintenance is established (Level 3). Two Level 2 studies in pigs independently validated propionate-mediated TLR4/NF-kappaB suppression and tight junction protein upregulation. Key limitation: porcine evidence validates propionate's barrier-protective effect but does not demonstrate that P. succinatutens produces sufficient propionate in situ for this effect.
modelSystem: Murine mono-colonization (L3); Porcine dietary fiber fermentation models (L2)
keyLimitation: Strain-specific causal validation missing in pigs: propionate's barrier effect validated, but P. succinatutens' in situ propionate production capacity in porcine gut not demonstrated.
summary: Best-validated probiotic function node. Dual-pathway propionate signaling mechanism fully characterized. Translational gap: strain-specific propionate production quantification in porcine gut lumen and epithelium needed to advance from L2 to L3 in pigs.

title: P. succinatutens: Lipid Deposition Reduction via MYC-Arginine-FOXO3a Axis
abstract: P. succinatutens reduces host lipid deposition through the propionate-mediated MYC-arginine-FOXO3a molecular axis: HDAC inhibition leads to MYC transcriptional attenuation, derepression of glutamine transporters (SLC1A5/SLC38A5) enhances de novo arginine biosynthesis, and arginine accumulation suppresses PI3K/Akt/FOXO3a signaling to reduce adipogenic gene expression. Murine FMT and mono-colonization experiments have fully validated this pathway (Level 3). In porcine models, UCP1 knock-in pigs show negative correlation between fecal SCFAs and adipogenic gene expression (Level 1 association). The pathway is not P. succinatutens-specific in the porcine context. Functional divergence from P. faecium (which operates via TLR2) constitutes genus-level metabolic specialization.
modelSystem: Murine FMT + mono-colonization (L3); Porcine UCP1 knock-in correlation (L1)
keyLimitation: Pathway validated in mice only; porcine evidence is SCFA-adipogenic gene correlation, not P. succinatutens-specific. Pathway conservation across commercial pig breeds is speculative.
summary: Demonstrates genus-level functional divergence (P. succinatutens vs P. faecium). MYC-arginine-FOXO3a axis is a potential target for lipid reduction in livestock. Porcine validation remains entirely at association level.

title: Butyrate/SCFAs: Compartmentalized Co-Metabolism Node (Priority P1)
abstract: Butyrate is the most extensively validated node in the compartmentalized co-metabolism network. Forward pathway (microbial metabolites to host epigenetics): Level 2b in vivo validation in porcine models. Xiong et al. (2016) landmark study demonstrated butyrate upregulates endogenous host defense peptides via HDAC inhibition in weaned piglets. Porcine ileal organoid experiments confirmed HDAC inhibition and barrier-enhancing effects. Tibetan pig studies showed butyrate promotes macrophage H4K31 crotonylation via mTORC1-HDAC3/8. Reverse pathway (host epigenetics to microbiota): only Level 1. Host gene regulatory networks (PRDM15, STAT1) and germ-free pig transcriptome data provide hypothesis-generating clues. Coupling: Level 0. Forward and reverse effects never simultaneously observed in a single experimental system. Measurement Depth: Level 0. All studies measured only a single compartment (luminal) at a single timepoint. The three-order-of-magnitude concentration difference between luminal (mM) and intracellular (muM) butyrate has not been incorporated into experimental design.
modelSystem: Porcine in vivo (weaned piglets, Tibetan pigs) + ileal organoids
keyLimitation: Forward pathway at L2b but reverse pathway at L1 and coupling at L0. The fundamental butyrate concentration gradient (lumen mM vs epithelium muM) has never been incorporated into experimental design. No study has simultaneously measured forward and reverse effects.
summary: Most-validated co-metabolism node. Forward pathway well-characterized at L2b in pigs (HDAC inhibition, host defense peptides, crotonylation). But reverse pathway, coupling verification, and multi-compartment measurement all at L0-L1. Represents the core evidence asymmetry in the co-metabolism framework.

title: Bile Acids: Host-Microbiome Signaling Node (Priority P2)
abstract: Bile acids are a porcine-validated core node. Forward pathway: Level 2b. Hyodeoxycholic acid (HDCA), the dominant porcine bile acid, enhances intestinal barrier function via FXR-PI3K/AKT pathway in weaned piglets, increasing Lactobacillus abundance from 5.28% to 37.97%. Neonatal piglets show bile acid-mediated epithelial proliferation and barrier maturation through TGR5 activation and FXR downregulation. Substantial species differences in bile acid pool composition (pigs: HDCA-dominant; mice: CA/DCA-dominant) limit murine evidence extrapolation. Reverse pathway: only Level 1. Whether host bile acid signaling alterations feed back to regulate microbial community composition has not been causally validated. Coupling: Level 0. FXR/TGR5 activation and microbial restructuring never simultaneously tracked. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Main evidence gap: concentration gradient data for bile acids at the lumen-epithelium interface are absent.
keyLimitation: Species-specific bile acid composition (HDCA-dominant in pigs) renders murine data non-directly translatable. Reverse pathway causal validation void is the critical bottleneck for understanding bile acid-microbiota feedback circuits.
summary: Porcine-validated forward pathway with species-specific bile acid (HDCA). Lactobacillus expansion from 5.3% to 38.0% is the strongest microbial community shift documented in any node. Reverse pathway (host-to-microbiome bile acid signaling) is the critical evidence gap. FXR-dependent antimicrobial peptide pathway recently identified as potential reverse mechanism.

title: Tryptophan Metabolites: AhR Signaling Node (Priority P2)
abstract: Tryptophan metabolites are partially porcine-validated. Forward pathway: Level 2a (preliminary in vivo). Bacteroides thetaiotaomicron alleviates weaning diarrhea in piglets via tryptophan metabolism-AhR-Nrf2 signaling. Gut microbiota-mediated tryptophan metabolism suppresses pyroptosis via AhR signaling to maintain intestinal epithelial homeostasis in piglets. Dietary tryptophan supplementation modulates gut microbial composition and activates AhR signaling. Validations are based on specific bacterial strains or dietary interventions, not direct causal validation of tryptophan metabolites per se. Reverse pathway: Level 0. Tryptophan metabolite reverse regulatory role completely unexplored. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Core evidence gap: concentration gradients of tryptophan metabolites (indole, kynurenine) across lumen-epithelium-microenvironment compartments never simultaneously measured. Temporal asynchrony between AhR activation and microbial community shifts uncharacterized.
keyLimitation: Validations are strain-specific (B. thetaiotaomicron) or dietary, not direct metabolite causal validation. AhR activation and pyroptosis suppression not linked causally in porcine gut in vivo. Reverse pathway entirely unexplored.
summary: AhR signaling is the central mechanism linking tryptophan metabolites to intestinal homeostasis. Strain-specific (B. thetaiotaomicron) and dietary intervention evidence at L2a. Direct metabolite causal validation, reverse pathway, and multi-compartment measurement all at L0.

title: Polyamines: Cross-Species Extrapolation Node (Priority P3)
abstract: Polyamines are a cross-species extrapolated predicted node with no direct validation evidence at any level in porcine models. Forward pathway: only Level 1. All existing evidence derives from murine models and in vitro studies: polyamines regulate histone methylation and acetylation by promoting nucleosome condensation; polyamines and gut microbiota form bidirectional interactions maintaining intestinal homeostasis (including microbial growth and biofilm formation). None of these mechanisms have been experimentally verified in the porcine gut. Reverse pathway: Level 0. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Core evidence gaps: baseline concentration and spatial distribution data for polyamines in the porcine gut are entirely absent; polyamine biosynthetic capacity of porcine-derived strains uncharacterized; polyamine transporter expression patterns in porcine intestinal epithelium unknown.
modelSystem: Murine models and in vitro only
keyLimitation: No porcine data at any level. Baseline polyamine concentrations in porcine gut unknown. Porcine-derived polyamine-producing strains not isolated. Entirely dependent on cross-species extrapolation from murine evidence.
summary: Weakest evidence base among all five nodes in porcine context. All evidence is murine-derived. Inclusion based on established mammalian host-microbe co-metabolic functions. Porcine functional relevance requires de novo establishment. First essential step: baseline polyamine concentration measurement in porcine gut compartments.

title: Vitamin B12: One-Carbon Metabolism Node (Priority P3)
abstract: Vitamin B12 has the weakest evidence base among all five nodes. Forward pathway: only Level 1 (association inference). B12 is a key cofactor in host one-carbon metabolism, participating in S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) synthesis to provide methyl donors for DNA methylation. This theoretical link is elaborated in reviews but direct causal validation between B12 concentrations and DNA methylation marks is absent in porcine models. In P. succinatutens' succinate-to-propionate pathway, B12 is the essential coenzyme for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase. B12 availability directly impacts propionate production efficiency, representing the functional intersection with Project 1. Reverse pathway: Level 0. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Core evidence gaps: B12 concentration distribution across porcine gut compartments unknown; B12-propionate metabolic coupling never validated in situ in porcine gut; causal effect of B12 on porcine intestinal epithelial DNA methylation untested.
modelSystem: Theoretical/review-level only; no direct experimental validation
keyLimitation: Weakest of all five nodes. Entirely at theoretical/review level. No experimental data on B12-DNA methylation link in porcine gut. B12-propionate coupling in situ completely untested. P. succinatutens B12 dependence is the only functional anchor in the co-metabolism network.
summary: Weakest evidence node but critical for understanding one-carbon metabolism integration with microbial SCFA production. Functional intersection with P. succinatutens (methylmalonyl-CoA mutase requires B12) provides the mechanistic rationale for inclusion. Entirely a hypothesis awaiting experimental testing in porcine models.
